pathophysiology_summary: Non-bullous impetigo is a superficial staphylococcal/streptococcal skin infection, often entering via a break in the skin (here under the nose after a URTI), producing rapidly spreading thick friable honey-coloured crusts on an erythematous base with a collarette of scale on removal, regional lymphadenopathy, and autoinoculation to new sites via the fingers. It is highly contagious. Extensive/facial involvement warrants oral plus topical antibiotics, with meticulous hygiene, exclusion from childcare/school (until 24h of antibiotics or lesions dry), no sharing of towels, and treatment of affected contacts. Discriminators: honey-coloured crusts without grouped clear vesicles or a neuropathic prodrome argue against HSV; absence of punched-out monomorphic erosions and underlying severe eczema argues against eczema herpeticum; purulent golden crusting with lymphadenopathy and rapid asymmetric spread argues against a geometric contact dermatitis. The must-not-miss is progression to facial/periorbital cellulitis (spread toward the eyes, hot hard redness, systemic features), which is absent here.

differential.evidence_basis: Honey-coloured crusts spreading around nose/mouth after a URTI, with autoinoculation.
Screen for spread toward the eyes/systemic features given the facial location.
Distinguish grouped vesicles/punched-out erosions from honey crusts.
Itchy geometric pattern without golden crusts/lymphadenopathy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Diagnose impetigo, screen for facial cellulitis and HSV, and stress hygiene/exclusion and contact treatment.

differential.evidence_basis: Honey crusts, no spread toward eyes/systemic features, not vesicular.
No spread toward the eyes, hot/hard redness or systemic toxicity — remains a safety-net.
Working diagnosis: oral + topical antibiotics, crust care, strict hygiene/exclusion and contact treatment; cellulitis safety-net.

red_flags.description: Facial/periorbital cellulitis or systemic toxicity (spread toward eyes, hot hard redness, high fever, lethargy, refusing all fluids)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Facial cellulitis near the eyes is serious (periorbital/orbital); systemic spread needs urgent care.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not screening for spread toward the eyes/systemic features in facial impetigo.
Not giving hygiene/exclusion advice or treating affected household contacts, leading to spread.
Mislabelling honey-crusted impetigo as HSV (or vice versa) and choosing the wrong therapy.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prescribe oral + topical antibiotics (weight-appropriate), crust care and strict hygiene/exclusion/contact treatment; urgent review/ED if spread toward the eyes or systemic features.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing early facial/periorbital cellulitis, or under-emphasising contagion control.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Localised impetigo: topical antibiotic; extensive/facial or multiple lesions: add oral antibiotics; emphasise hygiene, exclusion from school/childcare, and treat affected contacts.
